title: Token rewards permanently burned via address(0) transfer in claim()
claim: The claim() function calls token.transfer(address(0), ...) instead of token.transfer(msg.sender, ...), causing all user token rewards to be permanently burned. The commented-out //msg.sender on line 279 confirms the intended recipient was msg.sender. The token contract (ChainPaint741 at 0xdFa1A58780ef2208Cb55A1a77EcEE05E7a295397) explicitly handles to==address(0) as a burn via _burn(from, amount) in its _transfer override.
impact: All 2,534.88 PXL tokens held by the Game contract will be permanently destroyed when users claim their rewards. User 0x3259ab8450e06fC68b2CFd0FC2C69318C9081415 already claimed with 288/576 shares, resulting in 1,267.44 PXL burned on-chain. No rescue function exists; tokens cannot be recovered by anyone, including the owner (0x28D808550ED0a9A15bD3B9103664B8C12aBFA740).

paths.description: Any user calls claim() after auction ends; their proportional share of tokenToClaimTotal is transferred to address(0) and burned instead of sent to them.

title: Reentrancy in makeBid() allows previous bidder to drain auction ETH via CEI violation
claim: The makeBid() function violates the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: _sendEther() (line 235) forwards all available gas to the previous bidder via low-level .call() BEFORE updating bidAddress (line 237) and bidEther (line 238). A malicious previous bidder can re-enter makeBid() from their receive function. Since bidEther is not yet updated, newBidEtherMin() returns the old minimum (5% of old bidEther), and _sendEther sends the old bidEther again on each re-entry, allowing the attacker to extract bidEther multiple times while only paying the minimum increment per iteration.
impact: An attacker who is the previous highest bidder can repeatedly extract their bid amount (bidEther) while only paying the minimum increment (bidEther * 5/100) per iteration. With bidEther = 0.01 ETH and attacker's initial msg.value = 1 ETH, up to ~105 iterations are possible, extracting ~0.9975 ETH profit. The attacker also ends up as the highest bidder with their original msg.value, effectively stealing ETH from the contract.

paths.description: Attacker becomes the highest bidder, then calls makeBid() from a malicious contract whose receive() function re-enters makeBid() with the minimum increment (newBidEtherMin = bidEther * 5 / 100).
paths.trigger: 1. Attacker bids to become bidAddress; 2. Attacker calls makeBid() with large msg.value; 3. receive() re-enters makeBid() with newBidEtherMin()

title: Self-overwriting chunks extends game timer at zero cost and resets chunk price to base
claim: The _writeChunkPrice function (lines 155-161) returns 0 when data.owner == writer (self-overwrite), meaning writeChunks() charges cost=0 tokens for overwriting one's own chunks. However, _writeChunk still extends _gameEndTime by chunkWriteAddsGameSeconds (60 seconds) on line 98 and sets chunk.price to 0 on line 94. This allows indefinite game extension at zero cost and resets the price escalation mechanism, enabling circumvention of the 10% override price increase.
impact: A chunk owner can extend the game timer indefinitely by repeatedly calling writeChunks on their own chunks at zero token cost, preventing the game from ever ending. Additionally, resetting chunk prices to 0 allows bypassing the 10% price escalation: an owner can reset their chunk price, then have an accomplice buy it for the base startChunkWritePrice instead of the properly escalated price (oldPrice + 10%).

paths.description: Chunk owner calls writeChunks with their own chunk coordinates; cost=0 tokens, but _gameEndTime increases by chunkWriteAddsGameSeconds (60s) per chunk written.
paths.trigger: call writeChunks([{x: ownX, y: ownY, data: any}])

paths.description: Owner self-overwrites to reset chunk.price to 0, then accomplice buys it for startChunkWritePrice instead of the escalated price, then transfers ownership back.
paths.trigger: Owner calls writeChunks on own chunk (price→0) → accomplice calls writeChunks on same chunk (pays only startChunkWritePrice) → owner buys back
